Once the error model is computed, identical reads are dereplicated to reduce computational load, and the core `dada()` algorithm is applied to infer the true ASVs present in each sample.

-   **Learn Error Rates.** Iterates through each unique sequencing run to calculate its specific error model and generates diagnostic plots for quality control. This is a computationally intensive step.

    ```{r}
    # Initialize lists to store error models per run
    err_models_F <- list()
    err_models_R <- list()

    # Loop through each sequencing run to learn errors
    for (seq_run in unique(filter_files_per_primer$Run)) {
      
      message(paste0("Learning errors for Run: ", seq_run))
      
      # Get filtered file lists for this run
      seq_files <- filter_files_per_primer %>%
        dplyr::filter(Run == seq_run) %>%
        dplyr::filter(file.exists(FWD_R1_filtered) & file.exists(FWD_R2_filtered))

      seq_fnFs <- seq_files$FWD_R1_filtered
      seq_fnRs <- seq_files$FWD_R2_filtered
      
      # Learn Error Rates
      tictoc::tic("Learning error rates")
      
      message("  Learning forward errors...")
      err_models_F[[seq_run]] <- dada2::learnErrors(fls = seq_fnFs, 
                                                    multithread = TRUE, 
                                                    randomize = TRUE, 
                                                    verbose = TRUE)
                                     
      message("  Learning reverse errors...")                               
      err_models_R[[seq_run]] <- dada2::learnErrors(fls = seq_fnRs, 
                                                    multithread = TRUE, 
                                                    randomize = TRUE, 
                                                    verbose = TRUE)
      
      tictoc::toc()
      
      
      # Error plot R1
      err_plotF <- dada2::plotErrors(err_models_F[[seq_run]], nominalQ = TRUE) + 
        ggplot2::ggtitle(paste("Forward Errors -", seq_run))
        
      # Error plot R2
      err_plotR <- dada2::plotErrors(err_models_R[[seq_run]], nominalQ = TRUE) + 
        ggplot2::ggtitle(paste("Reverse Errors -", seq_run))
      
      # Save error plots for quality control review
      error_plot_path <- file.path(error_path, paste0(seq_run, "_error_rates.pdf"))
      
      ggplot2::ggsave(filename = error_plot_path,
                      plot = gridExtra::arrangeGrob(err_plotF, err_plotR, ncol = 2),
                      width = 12, height = 5)
                      
      message(paste0("  Error plots saved to: ", error_plot_path))
      
      # Open the plot
      utils::browseURL(error_plot_path)
    }
    ```

-   **Dereplicate and Infer ASVs.** Using the pre-calculated error models, identical reads are dereplicated to reduce computational load, and the core `dada()` algorithm is applied to infer the ASVs present in each sample.

    ```{r}
    # Initialize empty lists to store DADA2 output objects per sample
    all_dadaFs <- list()
    all_dadaRs <- list()
    all_derep_forward <- list()
    all_derep_reverse <- list()

    # Loop through each sequencing run for inference
    for (seq_run in unique(filter_files_per_primer$Run)) {
      
      message(paste0("Processing inference for Run: ", seq_run))
      
      # Get filtered file lists for this run
      seq_files <- filter_files_per_primer %>%
        dplyr::filter(Run == seq_run) %>%
        dplyr::filter(file.exists(FWD_R1_filtered) & file.exists(FWD_R2_filtered))

      seq_fnFs <- seq_files$FWD_R1_filtered
      seq_fnRs <- seq_files$FWD_R2_filtered
      names(seq_fnFs) <- seq_files$Unique_File_name_Primer
      names(seq_fnRs) <- seq_files$Unique_File_name_Primer

      # Dereplication
      message("  Dereplicating reads...")
      seq_derep_forward <- dada2::derepFastq(seq_fnFs, verbose = FALSE)
      seq_derep_reverse <- dada2::derepFastq(seq_fnRs, verbose = FALSE)
      
      # Sample Inference (DADA2 Algorithm) applying the specific run's error model
      message("  Inferring ASVs...")
      seq_dadaFs <- dada2::dada(seq_derep_forward, err = err_models_F[[seq_run]],
                                multithread = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)
      seq_dadaRs <- dada2::dada(seq_derep_reverse, err = err_models_R[[seq_run]],
                                multithread = TRUE, verbose = FALSE)
      
      # Append to global lists maintaining list hierarchy
      all_dadaFs <- append(all_dadaFs, seq_dadaFs)
      all_dadaRs <- append(all_dadaRs, seq_dadaRs)
      all_derep_forward <- append(all_derep_forward, seq_derep_forward)
      all_derep_reverse <- append(all_derep_reverse, seq_derep_reverse)
      }

    message("ASV Inference Complete across all runs.")
    ```

### Construct Sequence Table and Remove Chimeras

Following ASV inference, the independently processed forward and reverse reads must be recombined into a single, full-length amplicon sequence. DADA2 strictly requires a perfect overlap between the paired ends; reads that fail to align precisely due to poor quality or insufficient overlap length are discarded. Once the full amplicons are reconstructed, a critical filtration step is applied to remove chimeric sequences. Chimeras are artificial recombinations formed during PCR when an aborted amplicon extension acts as a primer in the next cycle, fusing segments of two distinct biological sequences. Because DADA2 resolves sequences at single-nucleotide precision, these chimeras are readily identified and removed, producing a pristine final sequence table.

-   **Merge paired reads.** Combine the inferred forward and reverse ASVs to reconstruct the full target amplicon. Only reads that perfectly overlap (without mismatches) by at least the specified minimum length are successfully merged.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Merging paired-end reads...")
      all_mergers <- dada2::mergePairs(dadaF = all_dadaFs,
                                       derepF = all_derep_forward,
                                       dadaR = all_dadaRs,
                                       derepR = all_derep_reverse,
                                       minOverlap = 10,
                                       maxMismatch = 0,
                                       verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Construct Sequence Table (ASV table)
      mergers_seqtab <- dada2::makeSequenceTable(all_mergers)
      
      # Check raw ASV length distribution
      message("Amplicon length distribution before chimera removal:")
      table(nchar(dada2::getSequences(mergers_seqtab)))
    }
    ```

-   **Concat unpaired reads.** Combine the inferred forward and reverse reads that lack sufficient overlap by artificially joining them. Instead of a true merge, this step inserts a spacer of 'N's between the read pairs, allowing the sequence information from both ends to be retained and analyzed as a single unit.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Concatenating unpaired reads...")
      all_concat <- dada2::mergePairs(dadaF = all_dadaFs,
                                      derepF = all_derep_forward,
                                      dadaR = all_dadaRs,
                                      derepR = all_derep_reverse,
                                      justConcatenate = TRUE,
                                      verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Construct Sequence Table (ASV table)
      concat_seqtab <- dada2::makeSequenceTable(all_concat)
      
      # Check raw ASV length distribution
      message("Amplicon length distribution before chimera removal:")
      table(nchar(dada2::getSequences(concat_seqtab)))
    }
    ```

-   **Construct Sequence Table (Separeted unmerged reads).** When paired-end reads do not sufficiently overlap to reconstruct the full amplicon, the analysis must proceed with R1 and R2 reads independently, generating separate ASV tables for each read direction.

    ```{r}
    # Construct Sequence Tables for unmerged reads.
    R1_seqtab <- dada2::makeSequenceTable(all_dadaFs)
    R2_seqtab <- dada2::makeSequenceTable(all_dadaRs)
    ```

-   **Remove chimeras.** Identify and remove artifactual sequences formed during PCR amplification when two different biological sequences combine. The `method="consensus"` compares sequences across samples to confidently detect these artifacts.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Identifying and removing chimeric sequences...")
      
      mergers_seqtab.nochim <- dada2::removeBimeraDenovo(mergers_seqtab,
                                                         method = "consensus",
                                                         multithread = TRUE,
                                                         verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Calculate chimera removal stats
      chimera_prop <- base::sum(mergers_seqtab.nochim) / base::sum(mergers_seqtab)
      message(paste0("Retained ", base::round(chimera_prop * 100, 2), 
                     "% of reads after chimera removal."))
      
      # Inspect final length distribution
      message("Final Amplicon length distribution (No Chimeras):")
      table(nchar(dada2::getSequences(mergers_seqtab.nochim)))
      
      # Save the final pristine sequence table as an R object to prevent data loss
      saveRDS(mergers_seqtab.nochim, file = file.path(nochim_path, "seqtab.nochim.rds"))
      message("Saved merged final sequence table to: ", file.path(nochim_path,
                                                                  "seqtab.nochim.rds"))
    }
    ```

-   **Remove chimeras (Concatenated reads).** As with the merged amplicons, the artificially joined sequences may contain chimeric artifacts generated during the PCR amplification steps.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Identifying and removing chimeric sequences from Concatenated reads...")
      
      concat_seqtab.nochim <- dada2::removeBimeraDenovo(concat_seqtab,
                                                        method = "consensus",
                                                        multithread = TRUE,
                                                        verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Calculate chimera removal stats
      chimera_prop_concat <- base::sum(concat_seqtab.nochim) / base::sum(concat_seqtab)
      message(paste0("Retained ", base::round(chimera_prop_concat * 100, 2), 
                     "% of reads after chimera removal."))
      
      # Inspect final length distribution
      message("Final Amplicon length distribution (No Chimeras):")
      table(nchar(dada2::getSequences(concat_seqtab.nochim)))
      
      # Save the final pristine sequence table as an R object to prevent data loss
      saveRDS(concat_seqtab.nochim, file = file.path(nochim_path, "concat_seqtab.nochim.rds"))
      message("Saved merged final sequence table to: ", file.path(nochim_path,
                                                                  "concat_seqtab.nochim.rds"))
    }
    ```

-   **Remove chimeras (Unmerged reads).** Just as with the merged sequences, the independent R1 and R2 sequence tables may contain chimeric amplicons generated during the PCR amplification steps.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Identifying and removing chimeric sequences from unmerged R1 and R2 reads...")
      
      # Remove chimeras from R1
      R1_seqtab.nochim <- dada2::removeBimeraDenovo(R1_seqtab, 
                                                  method = "consensus", 
                                                  multithread = TRUE, 
                                                  verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Calculate chimera removal stats for R1 reads
      chimera_prop_R1 <- base::sum(R1_seqtab.nochim) / base::sum(R1_seqtab)
      message(paste0("Retained ", base::round(chimera_prop_R1 * 100, 2), 
                     "% of R1 reads after chimera removal."))
      
      # Save the final pristine sequence table as an R object to prevent data loss
      saveRDS(R1_seqtab.nochim, file = file.path(nochim_path, "R1_seqtab.nochim.rds"))
      message("Saved R1 final sequence table to: ", file.path(nochim_path,
                                                              "R1_seqtab.nochim.rds"))

      # Remove chimeras from R2
      R2_seqtab.nochim <- dada2::removeBimeraDenovo(R2_seqtab, method = "consensus", 
                                                  multithread = TRUE, verbose = TRUE)
      
      # Calculate chimera removal stats for R2 reads
      chimera_prop_R2 <- base::sum(R2_seqtab.nochim) / base::sum(R2_seqtab)
      message(paste0("Retained ", base::round(chimera_prop_R2 * 100, 2), 
                     "% of R2 reads after chimera removal."))
      
      # Save the final pristine sequence table as an R object to prevent data loss
      saveRDS(R2_seqtab.nochim, file = file.path(nochim_path, "R2_seqtab.nochim.rds"))
      message("Saved R2 final sequence table to: ", file.path(nochim_path,
                                                              "R2_seqtab.nochim.rds"))
    }
    ```

### Track Read Retention

A fundamental quality control practice in metabarcoding is monitoring the proportion of reads that successfully survive each step of the bioinformatic pipeline. Extreme drops in read counts at specific stages often indicate technical issues. For instance, massive losses during the initial filtering step may point to poor sequencing quality or overly aggressive truncation parameters, whereas massive losses during the merging step usually indicate that the reads are too short to properly overlap (e.g., severe read-through or improper size selection). This section aggregates the sequence retention metrics from raw inputs through the final chimera removal, generating a comprehensive diagnostic table for auditing and reporting.

-   **Compile statistics.** Gather read counts from every processing step (Raw/Cutadapt → Filtered → Denoised → Merged → Non-chimeric) to assess pipeline quality and detect samples where massive data loss ocurred.

    ```{r}
    {
      message("Compiling read retention statistics across all pipeline stages...")
      
      # 1. Helper function to extract counts from DADA objects
      getN <- function(x) { base::sum(dada2::getUniques(x)) }
      
      # 2. Extract RAW read counts
      # We only count R1 because paired-end files have identical line counts
      raw_reads_paths <- sample_idx_tbl %>%
        dplyr::filter(Stage == "FWD_R1") %>%
        dplyr::pull(`Read file`, name = Unique_File_name)
      
      # 3. Extract Cutadapt read counts and build composite key
      # cut_files_per_primer contains Unique_File_name, we use unite() to match the DADA2 outputs
      tbl_Cutadapt <- cut_files_per_primer %>%
        dplyr::filter(Stage == "FWD_R1_cutadapt") %>%
        dplyr::mutate(`Primer detected reads (pairs)` = 
                        sapply(`Read file`, function(x) ShortRead::countFastq(x)[,1])) %>%
        tidyr::unite(col = "Unique_File_name_Primer",
                     Unique_File_name, Primer,
                     sep = "--",
                     remove = FALSE) %>%
        dplyr::select(Unique_File_name_Primer, 
                      Unique_File_name, Primer, 
                      `Primer detected reads (pairs)`)
      
      # 4. Extract DADA2 sequence steps
      tbl_Denoised_R1 <- tibble::enframe(sapply(all_dadaFs, getN),
                                         name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                         value = "Denoised (R1)")
      
      tbl_Denoised_R2 <- tibble::enframe(sapply(all_dadaRs, getN),
                                         name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                         value = "Denoised (R2)")
      
      tbl_Merged <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(mergers_seqtab),
                                         name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                         value = "Merged")
      
      tbl_Concat <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(concat_seqtab),
                                    name = "Unique_File_name_Primer",
                                    value = "Concat")
      
      tbl_NonChim <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(mergers_seqtab.nochim),
                                         name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                         value = "Non-chimeric Merged")
      
      tbl_NonChim_Concat <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(concat_seqtab.nochim),
                                            name = "Unique_File_name_Primer",
                                            value = "Non-chimeric Concat")
      
      tbl_NonChim_R1 <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(R1_seqtab.nochim), 
                                      name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                      value = "Non-chimeric R1")
      
      tbl_NonChim_R2 <- tibble::enframe(rowSums(R2_seqtab.nochim), 
                                      name = "Unique_File_name_Primer", 
                                      value = "Non-chimeric R2")
      
      # 4. Combine all tracking metrics into a single cohesive table
      all_track <- list(tbl_Cutadapt,
                        tbl_Denoised_R1,
                        tbl_Denoised_R2,
                        tbl_Merged,
                        tbl_Concat,
                        tbl_NonChim,
                        tbl_NonChim_Concat,
                        tbl_NonChim_R1, 
                        tbl_NonChim_R2) %>%
        purrr::reduce(dplyr::left_join, 
                      by = "Unique_File_name_Primer") %>%
        dplyr::left_join(pre_filtered_summary %>%  dplyr::select(-c(Proportion)), 
                         by = "Unique_File_name") %>%
        dplyr::left_join(filtered_summary %>% dplyr::select(-c(Proportion, `Raw reads (pairs)`)), 
                         by = "Unique_File_name") %>%
        # Join with original configuration metadata
        dplyr::left_join(primers_n_samples,
                         by = c("Unique_File_name", "Primer")) %>%
        # Reorder columns logically to match the sequence of events
        dplyr::relocate(Project, Sample, `Raw reads (pairs)`, `Pre-Filtered reads (pairs)`,
                        `Primer detected reads (pairs)`, `Filtered reads (pairs)`,
                        `Denoised (R1)`, `Denoised (R2)`, Merged, Concat, `Non-chimeric Merged`, 
                        `Non-chimeric Concat`, `Non-chimeric R1`, `Non-chimeric R2`)
      
      # Show preview
      all_track %>%
      dplyr::select(Unique_File_name, 
                    `Raw reads (pairs)`, 
                    `Pre-Filtered reads (pairs)`, 
                    `Primer detected reads (pairs)`,
                    `Filtered reads (pairs)`,
                    `Denoised (R1)`,
                    `Denoised (R2)`,
                    `Non-chimeric Merged`,
                    `Non-chimeric Concat`,
                    `Non-chimeric R1`,
                    `Non-chimeric R2`) %>%
      View()
    }
    ```

-   **Save metrics to sheet.** Export the aggregated tracking table to individual spreadsheets for project-specific reporting and external auditing.

    ```{r}
    {
      for (PRJ in all_projects) {
        
        prj_track <- all_track %>%
          dplyr::filter(Project == PRJ)
        
        output_file <- file.path(tables_path, paste0(PRJ, "-reads_and_seqs_counts-",
                                                     Sys.Date(), ".csv"))
        
        readr::write_csv(prj_track, file = output_file)
        }

      message("✓ Saved read tracking metrics to datasheet file.")
    }
    ```

-   **Plot Read Retention.** Transforms the tabular data into a long format to generate faceted bar plots. These visual summaries quickly highlight whether data loss occurred uniformly across stages or if specific samples failed abruptly at a particular step. Plots are saved automatically as PDFs.

    ```{r}
    # Transform tibble to long format for ggplot
    track_tbl <- all_track %>%
      tidyr::gather(key = "Stage", 
                    value = "Read counts",
                    "Raw reads (pairs)",
                    "Pre-Filtered reads (pairs)", 
                    "Primer detected reads (pairs)",
                    "Filtered reads (pairs)",
                    "Denoised (R1)",
                    "Denoised (R2)",
                    "Merged",
                    "Non-chimeric Merged",
                    "Non-chimeric Concat",
                    "Non-chimeric R1",
                    "Non-chimeric R2") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Stage = factor(Stage, levels = c("Raw reads (pairs)",
                                                     "Pre-Filtered reads (pairs)", 
                                                     "Primer detected reads (pairs)",
                                                     "Filtered reads (pairs)",
                                                     "Denoised (R1)",
                                                     "Denoised (R2)",
                                                     "Merged",
                                                     "Non-chimeric Merged",
                                                     "Non-chimeric Concat",
                                                     "Non-chimeric R1",
                                                     "Non-chimeric R2"))) 
      
    options(scipen = 22)

    # Define a clean Viridis palette explicitly mapping to our 7 stages
    stage_colors <- viridis::turbo(n = 11, direction = -1)

    for (PRJ in all_projects) {

      project_name <- stringr::str_replace_all(PRJ, " ", "_")
      
      # Filter for current project
      prj_data <- track_tbl %>% 
        dplyr::filter(Project == PRJ) %>%
        dplyr::arrange(Unique_File_name)

      N_samples <- length(unique(prj_data$Unique_File_name))

      track_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(prj_data, aes(y = Stage,
                                                  x = `Read counts`, 
                                                  fill = Stage,
                                                  group = Unique_File_name)) +
        ggplot2::geom_bar(stat = "identity", alpha = 0.85) + 
        ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = stage_colors) +
        ggplot2::labs(title = paste0("Project: ", PRJ),
                      subtitle = "Number of sequences per library across data processing stages",
                      x = "Number of sequences",
                      y = "Data processing stage") +
        ggplot2::facet_wrap(Unique_File_name ~ Primer, ncol = 8) +
        ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 8) +
        ggplot2::theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
                       legend.position = "bottom",
                       strip.text = element_text(size = 7)) +
        # Dynamic coordinate ratio to prevent squished bars
        ggplot2::coord_fixed(ratio = max(prj_data$`Read counts`, na.rm = TRUE) / length(unique(prj_data$Stage)) * 0.75)

      # Save plot
      plot_file <- file.path(figs_path, paste0(project_name, "-samples_track.pdf"))
      
      ggplot2::ggsave(file = plot_file,
                      plot = track_plot,
                      device = "pdf",
                      width = ifelse(N_samples <= 10, 8, 12),
                      height = ifelse(N_samples <= 10, 4, N_samples / 6),
                      units = "cm", 
                      scale = 5, 
                      limitsize = FALSE,
                      dpi = 600)
                      
      message(paste0("  Saved tracking plot for ", PRJ, ": ", basename(plot_file)))
      
      # Open the plot
      utils::browseURL(plot_file)
    }
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "3_DADA2_worflow-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 4. Phyloseq Integration & Data Structuring

Once ASVs are inferred and chimeras removed, the sequence counts must be integrated with the experimental metadata. In this section, we use the Phyloseq package to merge these different data types into a cohesive structure. Subsequently, we 'melt' this object into a tidy, long-format dataframe, calculate relative abundances, and visualize the ASV length distribution to assess data integrity prior to taxonomic assignment.

### Prepare Sample Metadata

Before creating the Phyloseq object, the sample metadata table must be formatted so that its row names exactly match the sample IDs of the DADA2 ASV sequence table. This code extracts the relevant columns from the configuration table, expands rows for samples with multiple primers, and validates the match between the two tables.

-   **Format metadata table.** Prepare the sample metadata by organizing identifiers and ensuring row names exactly match the sample names in the sequence table, a requirement for Phyloseq.

    ```{r}
    # Create sample metadata dataframe for Phylose
    # Attention! Add other metadata columns if needed
    all_samdf <- primers_n_samples %>% 
      dplyr::select("Project",
                    "Sample",
                    # "Researcher",
                    # "Analysis",
                    "Unique_File_name",
                    "Primer",
                    "Lib",
                    "Type",
                    # starts_with("Metadata"),
                    # "obs",
                    # "Extraction control",
                    # "Filtration control",
                    # "PCR control"
                    ) %>% 
      BiocGenerics::unique() %>%
      # Expand rows if multiple primers exist per sample (semi-colon separated)
      tidyr::separate_longer_delim(cols = Primer, delim = ";") %>%
      # Create unique identifier combining file name and primer
      tidyr::unite(col = "Unique_File_name_Primer",
                   Unique_File_name, 
                   Primer,
                   sep = "--",
                   remove = FALSE) %>%
      as.data.frame()

    # Assign row names to match ASV table dimensions
    rownames(all_samdf) <- all_samdf$Unique_File_name_Primer
    ```

-   **Check sample consistency.** Verify if all sequences in the ASV table have matching metadata in the newly created sample dataframe.

    ```{r}
    # Check consistency between sequence table and metadata
    seq_names <- rownames(mergers_seqtab.nochim)
    meta_names <- rownames(all_samdf)

    if (!all(seq_names %in% meta_names)) {
      warning("Caution: Some samples in the sequence table are missing from metadata!")
      print(setdiff(seq_names, meta_names))
    } else {
      message("All samples matched correctly between Sequence Table and Metadata.")
    }
    ```

### Build and format the Phyloseq Object

With validated metadata in hand, the ASV abundance table and sample data are combined into a single Phyloseq object. That object is then melted into a long-format tibble, column names are standardized, and relative abundances are calculated to allow fair comparisons across samples with unequal sequencing depths.

-   **Import DADA2 merged results.** Combine the ASV abundance table and the formatted sample metadata into a unified Phyloseq object.

    ```{r}
    # Create Phyloseq object from DADA2 merged results
    mergers_ps <- phyloseq::phyloseq(phyloseq::otu_table(mergers_seqtab.nochim,
                                                         taxa_are_rows = FALSE),
                                     phyloseq::sample_data(all_samdf))
    ```

-   **Import DADA2 unmerged results.** Combine the ASV abundance table and the formatted sample metadata into a unified Phyloseq object.

    ```{r}
    # Create Phyloseq object from DADA2 unmerged Concat reads
    concat_ps <- phyloseq::phyloseq(phyloseq::otu_table(concat_seqtab.nochim,
                                                    taxa_are_rows = FALSE),
                                phyloseq::sample_data(all_samdf))

    # Create Phyloseq object from DADA2 unmerged R1 reads
    R1_ps <- phyloseq::phyloseq(phyloseq::otu_table(R1_seqtab.nochim,
                                                    taxa_are_rows = FALSE),
                                phyloseq::sample_data(all_samdf))

    # Create Phyloseq object from DADA2 unmerged R2 reads
    R2_ps <- phyloseq::phyloseq(phyloseq::otu_table(R2_seqtab.nochim,
                                                    taxa_are_rows = FALSE),
                                phyloseq::sample_data(all_samdf))
    ```

-   **Transform merged data to tidy format.** Convert the Merged Reads Phyloseq object into a long-format dataframe (`psmelt`), filtering out zero-abundance ASVs to optimize memory usage and downstream processing.

    ```{r}
    # Convert Phyloseq object to long tibble
    # For merged data
    mergers_ps_tbl <- phyloseq::psmelt(mergers_ps) %>%
      tibble::as_tibble() %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Read origin` = "merged") %>%
      dplyr::filter(Abundance > 0) %>% # Filter strictly positive abundance
      dplyr::rename("ASV (Sequence)" = OTU) # Keep ASV sequence as identifier
    ```

-   **Transform unmerged data to tidy format.** Just as with the merged sequences, convert the Unmerged Phyloseq object into a long-format dataframe.

    ```{r}
    # Concat data
    concat_ps_tbl <- phyloseq::psmelt(concat_ps) %>%
      as_tibble() %>%
      mutate(`Read origin` = "Concat") %>%
      filter(Abundance > 0) %>%
      dplyr::rename("ASV (Sequence)" = OTU) 

    # R1 reads data
    R1_ps_tbl <- phyloseq::psmelt(R1_ps) %>%
      as_tibble() %>%
      mutate(`Read origin` = "R1") %>%
      filter(Abundance > 0) %>%
      dplyr::rename("ASV (Sequence)" = OTU) 

    # R2 reads data
    R2_ps_tbl <- phyloseq::psmelt(R2_ps) %>%
      as_tibble() %>%
      mutate(`Read origin` = "R2") %>%
      filter(Abundance > 0) %>%
      dplyr::rename("ASV (Sequence)" = OTU) 
    ```

-   **Standardize combined table.** Standardize column names and calculate the length of each ASV for plotting.

    ```{r}
    # Combine all processed tables (if multiple sources exist)
    all_ps_tbl <- mergers_ps_tbl %>%
      
      #Bind unmerged data
      # dplyr::bind_rows(concat_ps_tbl,
                       # R1_ps_tbl,
                       # R2_ps_tbl) %>%
      
      dplyr::select(-Sample) %>%  # Remove duplicate/confusing columns if necessary
      dplyr::rename(Sample = sample_Sample) %>% # Standardize Sample column name
      dplyr::mutate(`ASV Size (bp)` = nchar(`ASV (Sequence)`))

    # Show table
    View(all_ps_tbl)
    ```

-   **Compute relative abundances.** Calculate the proportion of each ASV relative to the sample's total library size and the overall dataset, enabling fair comparisons across libraries with unequal sequencing depths.

    ```{r}
    # Calculate relative abundances per sample and dataset
    all_ps_tbl <- all_ps_tbl %>%
      # Calculate Total Abundance of the entire dataset once
      dplyr::mutate(Dataset_Total_Abundance = sum(Abundance)) %>%
      # Group by sample context to calculate sample-specific totals
      dplyr::group_by(Project, 
                      Unique_File_name, 
                      Primer, 
                      `Read origin`) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(Sample_Total_Abundance = sum(Abundance),
                    Relative_Abundance_Dataset = round((Abundance /
                                                        Dataset_Total_Abundance),
                                                        digits = 6),
        Relative_Abundance_Sample  = round((Abundance / 
                                            Sample_Total_Abundance), 
                                            digits = 6)) %>%
      dplyr::ungroup() %>%
      # Clean up temporary total column
      dplyr::select(-Dataset_Total_Abundance) %>%
      dplyr::relocate(Sample_Total_Abundance, 
                      Relative_Abundance_Dataset, 
                      Relative_Abundance_Sample, 
                      .after = Abundance)

    # Show table
    View(all_ps_tbl)
    ```

### Check ASV Length Distribution

Before proceeding to taxonomic assignment, it is important to verify that the inferred ASVs fall within the expected size range for the target amplicon. The plot below displays ASV lengths across all samples, coloured by read origin and scaled by relative abundance, making it straightforward to spot outliers, unexpected size classes, or potential non-target amplicons.

-   **Visualize ASV lengths.** Plot the distribution of ASV sequence lengths prior to BLASTn identification. This diagnostic step helps flag unexpected size distributions or potential non-target amplicons.

    ```{r}
    # Plot ASV length distribution by sample
    asv_length_plot <- all_ps_tbl %>%
      ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(y = interaction(`Read origin`, 
                                                   Unique_File_name),
                                   x = `ASV Size (bp)`,
                                   fill = `Read origin`,
                                   col = `Read origin`,
                                   size = Relative_Abundance_Sample,
                                   alpha = 0.25)) +
      ggplot2::geom_jitter(height = 0.3, width = 0.3) +
      # ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(20, 
      #                                          max(all_ps_tbl$`ASV Size (bp)`, 
      #                                              na.rm = TRUE), 10),
      #                             expand = c(0.02, 0.02)) +
      ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = viridis::turbo(n = 12)[c(3, 6, 9, 12)]) +
      ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = viridis::turbo(n = 12)[c(3, 6, 9, 12)]) +
      ggplot2::labs(x = "ASV Size (bp)",
                    y = "Sample",
                    title = "ASV Length Distribution by Sample",
                    subtitle = "Size and Read Origin per Sample (all inferred ASVs)") +
      ggplot2::theme_bw(base_size = 8) +
      ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "right",
                     axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
                     strip.text.y = ggplot2::element_text(size = 10, angle = 0)) +
      # ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = c(10, max(all_ps_tbl$`ASV Size (bp)`, 
      #                                            na.rm = TRUE))) +
      ggplot2::facet_grid(rows = ggplot2::vars(Project, 
                                               Type),
                          cols = ggplot2::vars(Primer),
                          scales = "free_y",
                          space = "free_y")

    # Save plot
    ggplot2::ggsave(filename = file.path(figs_path, 
                                         "ASV_length_distribution-PreBLAST.pdf"),
                    plot = asv_length_plot,
                    device = "pdf",
                    width = 25,
                    height = 30,
                    units = "cm",
                    dpi = 600)

    # Open the plot
      utils::browseURL(file.path(figs_path, "ASV_length_distribution-PreBLAST.pdf"))
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "4_phyloseq_integr-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 5. BLASTr Taxonomic Assignment

Once the ASV table is validated and integrated into the Phyloseq object, each unique sequence must be assigned a taxonomic identity. This section performs that identification using BLASTn against one or more local nucleotide databases. Because the number of ASVs can be very large, queries are split into batches and executed in parallel using the BLASTr package. A correction step is also applied for custom databases (such as LGC12Sdb) that may lack standard NCBI TaxIDs.

### Prepare and Execute BLASTr

-   **Select unique ASVs.** This step extracts a deduplicated list of sequences, which is then split into manageable chunks for batch processing.

    ```{r}
    # Extract unique ASV sequences for BLAST submission
    asvs_blast_all <- all_ps_tbl %>%
      dplyr::pull(`ASV (Sequence)`) %>% 
      unique() %>% 
      stats::na.omit() %>% 
      as.character()
    ```

-   **Run Parallel BLAST.** Execute BLASTn in batches using the `BLASTr` package to handle large datasets efficiently without overloading memory.

    ```{r}
    # Define chunk size for batch processing (e.g., 20,000 sequences per batch)
    chunk_size  <- 50
    asv_chunks  <- split(asvs_blast_all, ceiling(seq_along(asvs_blast_all) / chunk_size))

    # Define local database paths
    db_paths <- "/data/databases/core_nt/core_nt /data/databases/LGC12Sdb/mai25/LGC12Sdb_complete_noGaps-2025-05-21.fasta"

    # Execute BLASTn across all chunks
    blast_results_list <- list()
    {
      tictoc::tic("Total BLASTn taxonomy assignment")
      
      for (i in seq_along(asv_chunks)) {

      message("Processing BLAST chunk ", i, " of ", length(asv_chunks))

      blast_results_list[[i]] <- BLASTr::parallel_blast(
        asv_chunks[[i]],
        db_path = db_paths,
        out_file = file.path(blast_path, paste0("blast_out_res_", i, ".csv")),
        out_RDS = file.path(blast_path, paste0("blast_out_res_", i, ".RDS")),
        total_cores = 60,       # Adjust based on server capacity
        num_threads = 2,
        blast_type = "blastn",
        perc_id = 80,
        perc_qcov_hsp = 80,
        num_alignments = 3
        )

      # Optional: Save intermediate environment after each chunk
      # base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "5_BLASTr-chunk", i, "-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
      }
      tictoc::toc()
    }
    ```

-   **Combine BLAST results.** Merge all chunk outputs into a single dataframe, removing rows where no subject was matched.

    ```{r}
    blast_res_full <- dplyr::bind_rows(blast_results_list) %>%
      dplyr::filter(!is.na(`1_subject header`)) %>%
      dplyr::rename(`ASV (Sequence)` = Sequence)

    message(nrow(blast_res_full), " BLAST hits retained across ", 
            length(unique(blast_res_full$`ASV (Sequence)`)), " unique ASVs.")  
    ```

### Correct TaxIDs

-   **Fill missing TaxID.** For hits lacking TaxIDs across all databases, extract genus/species names from subject headers and perform parallel NCBI lookups using `myTAI::taxonomy`, updating `1_staxid`, `2_staxid`, and `3_staxid` columns with success rates reported per hit.

    ```{r}
    # Helper function to retrieve TaxID from organism name
    get_taxid <- function(organism_name) {
      result <- tryCatch({
        myTAI::taxonomy(organism = organism_name,
                        db = "ncbi",
                        output = "taxid"
                        )$id[1]
        }, error = function(e) NA_character_)
      
      return(result)
    }

    # List of columns to process: (hit_number, staxid_column, subject_header_column, DB_column)
    hit_columns <- list(c(1, "1_staxid", "1_subject header", "1_DB"),
                        c(2, "2_staxid", "2_subject header", "2_DB"),
                        c(3, "3_staxid", "3_subject header", "3_DB"))

    # Process each hit (1st, 2nd, and 3rd)
    for (hit_info in hit_columns) {
      hit_num <- hit_info[1]
      staxid_col <- hit_info[2]
      subject_header_col <- hit_info[3]
      db_col <- hit_info[4]
      
      message("Processing TaxID lookup for hit ", hit_num, " (", staxid_col, ")")
      
      # Identify rows where TaxID is missing (NA or "N/A")
      missing_taxid_mask <- is.na(blast_res_full[[staxid_col]]) | 
                            blast_res_full[[staxid_col]] == "N/A"
      
      if (!any(missing_taxid_mask)) {
        message("  No missing TaxIDs found for ", staxid_col)
        next
      }
      
      # Filter only rows with missing TaxID
      missing_rows <- blast_res_full[missing_taxid_mask, ]
      
      message("  Found ", sum(missing_taxid_mask), " missing TaxIDs to lookup")
      
      # Extract organism_name from first word of subject header
      organism_names <- 
        stringr::word(missing_rows[[subject_header_col]], 1)
      
      # Lookup TaxIDs in parallel (uses furrr for acceleration)
      taxids <- 
        furrr::future_map_chr(organism_names,
                              get_taxid,
                              .options = furrr::furrr_options(seed = TRUE)
      )
      
      # Update only rows that had missing TaxID
      blast_res_full[[staxid_col]][missing_taxid_mask] <- taxids
      
      # Success report
      success_rate <- mean(!is.na(taxids)) * 100
      message("  Success rate: ", round(success_rate, 1), "% (", 
              sum(!is.na(taxids)), "/", length(taxids), " lookups)")
    }

    # Final summary
    message("\nFinal TaxID coverage:")
    message("Hit 1: ", round(mean(!is.na(blast_res_full$`1_staxid`)) * 100, 1), "%")
    message("Hit 2: ", round(mean(!is.na(blast_res_full$`2_staxid`)) * 100, 1), "%")
    message("Hit 3: ", round(mean(!is.na(blast_res_full$`3_staxid`)) * 100, 1), "%")
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "5_Tax_assigment-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 6. Taxonomy Curation

Following the Taxonomic Assignment, the raw taxonomic hits must be curated before they can be assigned to the final ASV dataset. This section refines the BLAST results by filtering out uninformative matches, standardizing species nomenclature, and retrieving complete taxonomic lineages from NCBI. Finally, missing intermediate taxonomic ranks are imputed to ensure a continuous hierarchy before joining the curated taxonomy back into the main Phyloseq-derived dataframe.

### Refine BLAST Hits

A single ASV query can return multiple alignments, some of which may point to uninformative database entries such as "Uncultured bacterium" or "environmental samples". This step evaluates the top three BLAST hits sequentially, discarding low-quality descriptions and selecting the best reliable identifier and its corresponding TaxID to represent the sequence.

-   **Filter low-quality hits.** Remove uninformative descriptions and select the best reliable hit among the top 3 alignments using a vectorized approach.

    ```{r}
    # Define patterns for non-informative hits
    bad_res_ids <- paste0(c("Uncultured", "Uncultured organism",
                            "Uncultured prokaryote",
                            "Eukaryotic synthetic construct",
                            "16S rRNA amplicon fragment",
                            "Invertebrate environmental", 
                            "Uncultured Candidatus", "Uncultured bacterium",
                            "Uncultured archaeon", 
                            "Complete Metagenome-Assembled", 
                            "PREDICTED: Nomascus"), collapse = "|")

    # Vectorized selection of best hit (1st, 2nd, or 3rd)
    blast_res_processed <- blast_res_full %>%
      dplyr::mutate(is_bad_1 = stringr::str_detect(`1_subject header`,
                                                   bad_res_ids),
                    is_bad_2 = stringr::str_detect(`2_subject header`,
                                                   bad_res_ids),
                    is_bad_3 = stringr::str_detect(`3_subject header`,
                                                   bad_res_ids),
    # Select best valid hit
    Selected_Hit_Origin = 
      dplyr::case_when(!is_bad_1 ~ "1", is_bad_1 & !is.na(`2_subject header`)
                       & !is_bad_2 ~ "2", is_bad_1 & is_bad_2 
                       & !is.na(`3_subject header`) & !is_bad_3 ~ "3",
                       TRUE ~ "None"),

    # Assign Final ID and TaxID based on selection
    `blast ID` = dplyr::case_when(Selected_Hit_Origin == "1" ~
                                    substr(`1_subject header`, 1, 40),
                       Selected_Hit_Origin == "2" ~
                         substr(`2_subject header`, 1, 40),
                       Selected_Hit_Origin == "3" ~ 
                         substr(`3_subject header`, 1, 40),
                       TRUE ~ "Match_not_reliable"),

    query_taxID = dplyr::case_when(Selected_Hit_Origin == "1" ~ `1_staxid`,
                                   Selected_Hit_Origin == "2" ~ `2_staxid`,
                                   Selected_Hit_Origin == "3" ~ `3_staxid`,
                                   TRUE ~ NA_character_)) %>%
      # Second pass clean-up for any lingering "Uncultured" that slipped through
      dplyr::mutate(`blast ID` = dplyr::if_else(
        stringr::str_detect(`blast ID`, "ncultur|nvironmental"),
        "Match_not_reliable",
        `blast ID`))

    # Check results
    table(blast_res_processed$Selected_Hit_Origin)
    ```

### Clean Species Names

Raw database headers often contain structural artifacts, strain identifiers, or taxonomic qualifiers (e.g., "cf.", "aff.", "MAG:"). To facilitate downstream ecological analyses and clear plotting, this step cleans the selected `blast ID` strings and reduces them strictly to binomial nomenclature (Genus species), while allowing for targeted manual corrections.

-   **Standardize nomenclature.** Clean up species names by removing artifacts, tags (e.g., "cf.", "aff."), and reducing names to Binomial format (Genus species).

    ```{r}
    # Clean strings
    blast_res_processed <- blast_res_processed %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`blast ID` = `blast ID` %>%
    # 1. Remove textual noise, punctuation, and line breaks from NCBI
        stringr::str_replace_all(
          c("Uncultured" = "", "uncultured" = "", "Candidatus" = "", 
          "MAG:" = "", "MAG TPA_asm:" = "", "TPA_asm:" = "", 
          "^Cf. " = "", "candidate division" = "", 
          "\\[" = "", "\\]" = "", "'" = "", "," = "", "\\n" = "")
          ) %>%
    # 2. Remove intermediate tags common in uncertain taxonomy
        stringr::str_replace_all(c(" cf. " = " ", " nr. " = " ")) %>%
    # 3. Cleans extra spaces (leading, trailing, and double spaces in between)
      stringr::str_squish() %>%
      # 4. Extracts Genus and Species
      stringr::word(1, 2, sep = stringr::fixed(" "))) %>%
      # 5. Forces confusing identifications into the desired standard
      dplyr::mutate(
    `blast ID` = dplyr::recode(`blast ID`,
      "Human DNA" = "Homo sapiens",
      "Human chromosome" = "Homo sapiens",
      "Eukaryotic synthetic" = "Homo sapiens"
    )
      )

    # Verify cleaned names
    head(unique(blast_res_processed$`blast ID`))
    ```

### Retrieve Complete Lineages

With reliable TaxIDs established, the pipeline queries NCBI to download the full taxonomic hierarchy for each identified organism. This ensures that every ASV is associated not just with a species name, but with its complete kingdom-to-species lineage, which is essential for taxonomic filtering and community analysis.

-   **Fetch complete taxonomy.** Query NCBI in parallel using `BLASTr::parallel_get_tax` to retrieve full lineage information based on the curated TaxIDs.

    ```{r}
    # Identify unique TaxIDs to query
    taxids_to_search <- unique(na.omit(blast_res_processed$query_taxID))

    # Execute parallel taxonomy retrieval
    taxonomy_tbl <- 
      BLASTr::parallel_get_tax(organisms_taxIDs = taxids_to_search,
                               retry_times = 5,
                               verbose = "full") %>%
      BiocGenerics::unique()

    # Check for missing IDs (failed queries)
    missing_taxids <- setdiff(taxids_to_search, taxonomy_tbl$query_taxID)
    if (length(missing_taxids) > 0) {
      message(paste(length(missing_taxids), "TaxIDs could not be retrieved."))
    }

    # Show Complete Taxonomy Table
    View(taxonomy_tbl)
    ```

### Fill Missing Taxonomic Ranks

Biological databases occasionally lack designations for certain intermediate ranks (e.g., a known Order but an undefined Family). To prevent `NA` values from fracturing downstream abundance plots, missing ranks are dynamically imputed by propagating information downwards from the nearest available higher rank. Finally, the completed taxonomy is joined back into the core ASV metadata table.

-   **Correct incomplete taxonomy.** Impute missing Genus entries by extracting them from the scientific name, and assign unresolved sequences to their proper Superkingdom (Archaea, Bacteria, Eukaryota, or Viruses) based on known Kingdom or Phylum affiliations.

    ```{r}
    taxonomy_tbl_filled <- taxonomy_tbl %>%
      
      # Fill missing Genus entries by extracting the first word from the Sci_name
      dplyr::mutate(
        `Genus (NCBI)` = dplyr::case_when(
          is.na(`Genus (NCBI)`) & !is.na(Sci_name) ~ stringr::word(Sci_name, 1),
          TRUE ~ `Genus (NCBI)`),
         
        # Manually assign missing Superkingdoms based on specific Kingdoms and Phyla
        `Superkingdom (NCBI)` = dplyr::case_when(
          !is.na(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`) ~ `Superkingdom (NCBI)`,
          
          # Assign Archaea
          `Kingdom (NCBI)` %in% c("Promethearchaeati", "Nanobdellati",
                                  "Methanobacteriati", "Thermoproteati") ~ "Archaea",
          
          # Assign Eukaryota
          `Kingdom (NCBI)` %in% c("Viridiplantae", "Metazoa", "Fungi") ~ "Eukaryota",
          `Phylum (NCBI)` %in% c("Ciliophora", "Euglenozoa", "Oomycota",
                                 "Bacillariophyta", "Tubulinea", "Apicomplexa",
                                 "Cercozoa", "Endomyxa", "Haptophyta", 
                                 "Hemimastigophora", "Preaxostyla", "Haptophyta",
                                 "Fornicata", "Rhodophyta", "Haptophyta", "Discosea",
                                 "Heterolobosea", "Nibbleridia","Parabasalia",
                                 "Perkinsozoa", "Foraminifera", "Telonemia", 
                                 "Nebulidia", "Evosea") ~ "Eukaryota",
          
          # Assign Bacteria
          `Kingdom (NCBI)` %in% c("Pseudomonadati", "Bacillati", "Fusobacteriati",
                                  "Thermotogati") ~ "Bacteria",
          stringr::str_detect(`Phylum (NCBI)`, "bacteriota$|bacterota$|bacteria$") ~
            "Bacteria",
          stringr::str_detect(`Phylum (NCBI)`, "Altimarinota$|Moduliflexota$|Microgenomatota$|Binatota$|CPR2$|CPR3$|Methylomirabilota$|Sumerlaeota$") ~ "Bacteria",
          
          # Assign Viruses
          `Kingdom (NCBI)` %in% c("Heunggongvirae") ~ "Viruses",
          stringr::str_detect(`Phylum (NCBI)`, "WWE3$") ~ "Viruses",
          
          TRUE ~ `Superkingdom (NCBI)`))

    # Show Complete Taxonomy Table
    View(taxonomy_tbl_filled)
    ```

-   **Fill missing taxonomic ranks.** Prevent gaps in the taxonomic lineage by sequentially propagating information downwards. Missing ranks are replaced with informative placeholders derived from the nearest available higher rank.

    ```{r}
    # Define the strict hierarchical order of taxonomic ranks to be filled
    tax_ranks <- c("Superkingdom (NCBI)", "Kingdom (NCBI)", "Phylum (NCBI)", 
                   "Subphylum (NCBI)", "Class (NCBI)", "Subclass (NCBI)", 
                   "Order (NCBI)", "Suborder (NCBI)", "Family (NCBI)", 
                   "Subfamily (NCBI)", "Genus (NCBI)")

    # Transform empty strings and literal "NA" values to true R NA before filling
    taxonomy_tbl_filled <- taxonomy_tbl_filled %>%
      dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(tax_ranks),
                                  ~ dplyr::na_if(., "") %>% dplyr::na_if("NA")))

    # Sequentially fill missing ranks based on the highest available adjacent rank
    for (i in 2:length(tax_ranks)) {
      current_rank <- tax_ranks[i]
      higher_rank <- tax_ranks[i - 1]
      
      # Extract the clean rank name for the prefix (e.g., "Phylum (NCBI)" -> "phylum")
      rank_prefix <- stringr::str_to_lower(stringr::word(current_rank, 1))
      
      taxonomy_tbl_filled <- taxonomy_tbl_filled %>%
        dplyr::mutate(!!rlang::sym(current_rank) := 
                        dplyr::coalesce(!!rlang::sym(current_rank),
                                        paste0(rank_prefix, " of ", 
                                               !!rlang::sym(higher_rank))
      )
    )
    }

    # Fix the edge case where Superkingdom might be NA but Kingdom is not
    taxonomy_tbl_filled <- taxonomy_tbl_filled %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Superkingdom (NCBI)` = dplyr::coalesce(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`,
                                                            paste0("superkingdom of ",
                                                                   `Kingdom (NCBI)`)))

    # Show Complete Taxonomy Table
    View(taxonomy_tbl_filled)
    ```

### Integrate Taxonomy and Abundance Data

Once the taxonomic lineages are fully resolved and curated, they must be merged back into the quantitative ecological data.

-   **Combine tax and BLAST hits.** Merge the curated sequence identifications with their fully resolved taxonomic lineages into a single reference table. This table is saved locally as an `.rds` object to serve as a persistent taxonomic database, preventing the need to re-query NCBI APIs if downstream steps fail.

    ```{r}
    # 1. Join taxonomy back to the processed BLAST results
    blast_res_tax <- blast_res_processed %>%
      dplyr::left_join(taxonomy_tbl_filled, 
                       by = "query_taxID") %>% 
      dplyr::select(-c(dplyr::starts_with("is_bad_"),
                       `Sci_name`)) %>% 
      dplyr::relocate(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`,
                      `Kingdom (NCBI)`,
                      `Phylum (NCBI)`,
                      `Subphylum (NCBI)`,
                      `Class (NCBI)`,
                      `Subclass (NCBI)`,
                      `Order (NCBI)`,
                      `Suborder (NCBI)`,
                      `Family (NCBI)`,
                      `Subfamily (NCBI)`,
                      `Genus (NCBI)`,
                      `blast ID`)
      
    # 2. Save a backup of the completed taxonomic assignment
    # Creating a dedicated RDS ensures we don't lose the API results
    saveRDS(object = blast_res_tax,
            file = file.path(blast_path, paste0("blast_res_tax_", Sys.Date(), ".rds")))

    # Optional: Reload if needed
    # blast_res_tax <- readRDS(file.path(blastpath, paste0("blast_res_tax_", Sys.Date(), ".rds")))
    ```

-   **Join taxonomy to sequence abundance.** Integrate the complete taxonomic reference table back into the core Phyloseq-derived dataframe (`all_ps_tbl`). This finalizes the dataset by linking every sequence variant to its respective sample metadata, read abundance, and biological identity.

    ```{r}
    # Link taxonomy to abundance results and filter out zeros
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl %>%
      dplyr::left_join(blast_res_tax %>%
                         dplyr::select(`ASV (Sequence)`, 
                                       `blast ID`,
                                       ends_with("(NCBI)")),
                       by = "ASV (Sequence)") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Final ID` = `blast ID`) %>%
      dplyr::filter(Abundance > 0)
      
    # Verify the final column structure
    colnames(all_ps_tbl_final)

    # Show Complete Results Table
    View(all_ps_tbl_final)
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "6_Tax_curation-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 7. BOLD Taxonomy Assignment

While BLASTn against local nucleotide databases covers most markers, CO1 barcodes often benefit from complementary identification using the BOLD Systems database. This optional step queries the BOLD API for selected ASVs, parses the top hits, and prepares a clean, wide-format table that can later be merged into the main results.

### Prepare execution

-   **Identify ASVs to query in BOLD.** Select CO1 (or other relevant) ASVs that are long enough and potentially under-identified by BLASTn, and generate their reverse complements for robust matching.

    ```{r}
    # Identify ASVs to be queried against BOLD (e.g., COI only)
    asvs_bold <- asvs_blast_all[nchar(asvs_blast_all) >= 80]

    # Also prepare reverse-complemented versions for completeness
    asvs_bold_rev <- as.character(
      Biostrings::reverseComplement(Biostrings::DNAStringSet(asvs_bold)))
    ```

-   **Define the BOLD query function.** Wrap the BOLD API call in a helper that retrieves the top Nhits, fetches taxonomy, and returns a tidy, wide-format result with basic metadata.

    ```{r}
    # Helper function to query BOLD and tidy the output
    ID_bold <- function(sequence,
                        db = "COX1_SPECIES_PUBLIC",
                        keepSeq = TRUE,
                        Nhits = 3) {
      # 1. Identify sequence via BOLD
      bold_res <- bold::bold_identify(sequences = c(sequence),
                                      db = db,
                                      response = FALSE,
                                      keep_seq = keepSeq)
      
      # Bail out early if no hits returned
      if (nrow(bold_res[[1]]) < 1) {
        return(tibble::tibble())
        }
      
      # 2. Filter first Nhits best hits by similarity
      bold_res_filt <- bold_res[[1]] %>%
        tibble::as_tibble() %>%
        dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(similarity)) %>%
        dplyr::slice_head(n = Nhits)
      
      # 3. Recover taxonomy for filtered hits
      bold_tax <- bold::bold_identify_taxonomy(bold_res_filt)
      
      # 4. Keep only the most informative fields and rename them
      bold_tax_filt <- bold_tax %>%
        dplyr::select(ID,
                      similarity,
                      taxonomic_identification,
                      genus_name,
                      family_name,
                      order_name,
                      class_name,
                      phylum_name,
                      sequence_descriptions,
                      database,
                      specimen_country) %>%
        dplyr::rename(`BOLD ID` = ID,
                      `BOLD similarity` = similarity,
                      `BOLD Species` = taxonomic_identification,
                      `BOLD Genus` = genus_name,
                      `BOLD Family` = family_name,
                      `BOLD Order` = order_name,
                      `BOLD Class` = class_name,
                      `BOLD Phylum` = phylum_name,
                      `BOLD Database` = database,
                      `BOLD Country` = specimen_country)
      
      # 5. Add a row index and pivot wider
      bold_tax_wide <- bold_tax_filt %>%
        tibble::rowid_to_column(var = "res") %>%
        tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = res,
                           values_from = dplyr::everything(),
                           names_glue = "hit{res}.value{.value}") %>%
        dplyr::mutate(Sequence = sequence) %>%
        dplyr::relocate(Sequence)
      
      return(bold_tax_wide)
      }
    ```

### Execute BOLD

-   **Run BOLD queries in parallel.** Use furrr to submit multiple sequences to BOLD and combine all returned hits into a single table, saving the result for reuse.

    ```{r}
    # Optional: run BOLD identification in parallel for all selected ASVs
    cores_to_use <- future::availableCores() - 2
    future::plan(future::multisession, workers = cores_to_use)

    BOLD_ids <- 
      furrr::future_map_dfr(.x = asvs_bold,
                            .f = ~ ID_bold(sequence = .x,
                                           db = "COX1_SPECIES_PUBLIC",
                                           keepSeq = TRUE,
                                           Nhits = 3),
                            .options = furrr::furrr_options(seed = NULL))

    # Save BOLD results for reproducibility
    saveRDS(object = BOLD_ids,
            file = file.path(blastpath, 
                             paste0("BOLD_ids_", Sys.Date(), ".rds")))
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "7_BOLD_Tax_assgnmnt-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 8. SWARM OTU Clustering

Although DADA2 produces exact sequence variants (ASVs), biological reality often involves intra-species variation or sequencing error thresholds that warrant clustering into Operational Taxonomic Units (OTUs). SWARM implements a local clustering algorithm that groups ASVs differing by exactly 1 nucleotide, producing a more conservative, ecologically meaningful partition of the data.

### Prepare input FASTA file

-   **Prepare ASV FASTA with headers.** Create a FASTA file of all non-zero ASVs with standardized headers (ASV001_150bp) containing size and total abundance information for traceability.

    ```{r}
    # Extract unique ASVs with abundance summaries
    asvs_abd <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::filter(!stringr::str_detect(`ASV_tip`, 
                                         pattern = "N")) %>%
      dplyr::select(`ASV_tip`, 
                    `ASV Sequence`, 
                    Abundance) %>%
      dplyr::group_by(`ASV_tip`, 
                      `ASV Sequence`) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`ASV total abundance` = sum(Abundance)) %>%
      dplyr::ungroup() %>%
      dplyr::select(`ASV_tip`, 
                    `ASV Sequence`, 
                    `ASV total abundance`) %>%
      dplyr::distinct() %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`ASV header` = paste0(`ASV header`, 
                                          "_", 
                                          `ASV total abundance`))

    # Write FASTA file with abundance-aware headers
    allasv_fasta_abd <- data.frame(header = asvs_abd$`ASV header`,
                                   sequence = asvs_abd$`ASV Sequence`)

    writeXStringSet(XStringSet = DNAStringSet(allasv_fasta_abd$sequence),
                    filepath = file.path(swarmpath, 
                                         paste0("analysis-", 
                                                Sys.Date(), 
                                                "-ASVs_abd.fasta")))
    ```

### Execute SWARM

-   **Execute SWARM clustering.** Call SWARM via command line to cluster ASVs into OTUs using the default 1 nucleotide distance threshold, generating representative sequences and cluster structure files.

    ```{r}
    # Run SWARM via system call (requires SWARM binary in PATH)
    swarm_cmd <- paste0("swarm -t 50 ",
                        file.path(swarmpath, 
                                  paste0("analysis-", 
                                         Sys.Date(),
                                         "-ASVs_abd.fasta")),
                        " -s ", file.path(swarmpath,
                                          paste0("analysis-",
                                                 Sys.Date(),
                                                 "-swarm.stats")),
                        " -o ", file.path(swarmpath,
                                          paste0("analysis-",
                                                 Sys.Date(),
                                                 "-swarm.out")),
                        " -w ", file.path(swarmpath,
                                          paste0("analysis-",
                                                 Sys.Date(),
                                                 "-swarm-representativeOTUs.fasta")),
                        " -i ", file.path(swarmpath,
                                          paste0("analysis-",
                                                 Sys.Date(),
                                                 "-swarm.structure")),
                        " -f")

    system2(command = "swarm", args = swarm_cmd)

    # Verify SWARM completed successfully
    swarm_out <- list.files(path = swarmpath, pattern = "swarm\\.out", full.names = TRUE)
    if (length(swarm_out) > 0) {
      message("SWARM clustering completed successfully!")
    } else {
      stop("SWARM failed. Check swarmpath and binary installation.")
    }
    ```

-   **Parse SWARM output and assign OTUs.** Read the cluster file and map each ASV back to its OTU number, creating a clean lookup table for integration into the main results.

    ```{r}
    # Read SWARM cluster output
    swarm_clusters <- readr::read_lines(swarm_out)

    # Helper function to parse OTU assignments from SWARM output
    find_OTU <- function(ASV_header, clusters = swarm_clusters) {
      ASV_clean <- stringr::str_remove_all(ASV_header, 
                                           pattern = ", .*")
      OTU_num <- which(grepl(ASV_clean, 
                             clusters)) - 1  # SWARM uses 0-based indexing
      tibble::tibble(`ASV header` = ASV_header,
                     OTU = OTU_num)
      }

    # Map OTUs to all ASVs in parallel
    cores_to_use <- future::availableCores() - 2
    future::plan(future::multisession, workers = cores_to_use)

    ASVs_and_OTUs <- furrr::future_map_dfr(.x = asvs_abd$`ASV header`,
                                           .f = find_OTU,
                                           .options = furrr::furrr_options(seed = NULL))

    # Generate unique OTU names and update headers
    sprt_dg <- nchar(length(unique(ASVs_and_OTUs$OTU)))
    ASVs_and_OTUs <- ASVs_and_OTUs %>%
      dplyr::mutate(OTU = paste0(sprintf(paste0("OTU%0", 
                                                sprt_dg, 
                                                "d"),
                                         OTU)),
                    `ASV header` = stringr::str_replace(`ASV header`, 
                                                        pattern = ", .*",
                                                        replacement = OTU))

    # Join OTU information back to ASV abundance summary
    asvs_abd <- dplyr::left_join(asvs_abd,
                                 ASVs_and_OTUs[, c("ASV header", "OTU")],
                                 by = "ASV header")

    message("SWARM clustering complete. Generated ",
            length(unique(ASVs_and_OTUs$OTU)), " OTUs.")
    ```

### Integrate results

-   **Integrate OTUs into main results table.** Add the OTU column to all_ps_tbl_final for downstream analysis.

    ```{r}
    # Join OTU assignments back to the main abundance table
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::left_join(asvs_abd[, c("ASV_tip", "OTU")],
                       by = "ASV_tip")

    # Verify integration
    table(all_ps_tbl_final$OTU, useNA = "always")
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "8_OTU_Clustring-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 9. Contamination Analysis

This step identifies ASVs present in negative controls (PCR, Extraction, Filtration blanks) and quantifies their relative abundance in environmental samples. For each ASV-sample pair, the maximum abundance observed in the corresponding control type is used as denominator to compute a Fold Change ratio. ASVs where sample abundance does not substantially exceed control levels are flagged as "Possible contamination".

### Identify ASVs in controls

-   **Identify ASVs in controls.** Extract all ASVs detected in any control sample and calculate their maximum relative abundance per control type.

    ```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
    # Define keywords for control sample types
    ctrl_types <- c("Extraction control", "Extraction Control", "Ext. control",
                    "Ext. Control", "Filt. Control", "PCR control", 
                    "PCR Control", "Filt. control", "Filtration control",
                    "Filtration Control", "Negative control", "Negative Control",
                    "Positive control", "Positive Control", "Control")

    # Extract ASVs present in ANY control
    all_contam_ASVs <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::filter(Abundance > 0, Type %in% ctrl_types) %>%
      dplyr::group_by(`ASV Sequence`, UniqueFilename) %>%
      dplyr::summarise(`Max. ASV abd. in control` =
                         max(`RelativeAbundanceSample`),
                       `Final ID BLASTn` = first(`Final ID BLASTn`),
                       .groups = "drop") %>%
      dplyr::select(UniqueFilename, 
                    `ASV Sequence`, 
                    `Max. ASV abd. in control`, 
                    `Final ID BLASTn`)

    message("Found ", nrow(all_contam_ASVs), " ASVs in controls across ",
            length(unique(all_contam_ASVs$UniqueFilename)), " control samples.")
    ```

### Calculate Fold Changes

-   **Calculate sample-to-control Fold Change ratios.** For each ASV in each environmental sample, compute the Fold Change against the maximum abundance observed in its corresponding control(s), marking potential contaminants.

    ```{r}
    # Add Fold Change columns to main table (initialized to 0)
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Prop. to PCR control` = 0,
                    `Prop. to Ext control` = 0, 
                    `Prop. to Filt control` = 0)

    # Extract control names from sample metadata
    extract_controls <- function(control_str) {
      
      if (is.na(control_str) || control_str == "") return(character(0))
      
      stringr::str_split(control_str, pattern = ",|;")[[1]]
      }

    # Calculate Fold Changes vectorized (replaces legacy line-by-line loop)
    for (ctrl_type in c("PCR control", 
                        "Extraction control", 
                        "Filtration control")) {
      
      # Get samples with this control type
      sample_controls <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
        dplyr::filter(Type %in% ctrl_types) %>%
        dplyr::mutate(ctrl_samples = purrr::map(`{{ctrl_type}}`, 
                                                extract_controls)) %>%
        tidyr::unnest(cols = ctrl_samples) %>%
        dplyr::select(`ASV Sequence`, 
                      ctrl_samples, 
                      `Max. ASV abd. in control`)
      
      # Compute Fold Change for environmental samples only
      all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
        dplyr::left_join(sample_controls,
                         by = c("ASV Sequence"),
                         suffix = c("", "_ctrl")) %>%
        dplyr::group_by(`ASV Sequence`, Unique_File_name) %>%
        dplyr::mutate(!!paste0("Prop. to ", 
                               ctrl_type) := dplyr::case_when(
                                 !is.na(`Max. ASV abd. in control_ctrl`) ~ 
                                   gtools::foldchange(
                                     `Relative_Abundance_Sample`,
                                     `Max. ASV abd. in control_ctrl`),
                                 TRUE ~ 0)) %>%
        dplyr::ungroup() %>%
        dplyr::select(-`Max. ASV abd. in control_ctrl`, -ctrl_samples)
      }
    ```

### Set contamination status

-   **Flag contamination status.** Apply legacy logic: mark as "Possible contamination" if ANY Fold Change ≤ 1, otherwise "True detection".

    ```{r}
    # Flag contamination 
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Contamination status` = dplyr::case_when(
        `Prop. to PCR control` <= 1 ~ "Possible contamination",
        `Prop. to Ext control` <= 1 ~ "Possible contamination",
        `Prop. to Filt control` <= 1 ~ "Possible contamination",
        TRUE ~ "True detection"))

    # Verify results match legacy expectations
    table(all_ps_tbl_final$`Contamination status`)
    table(all_ps_tbl_final$`Prop. to PCR control`, useNA = "always")
    ```

### Recalculate Clean Abundances

Once contamination statuses are flagged, these artifacts must be effectively removed from the quantitative data. This step zeroes the absolute abundance of any ASV identified as a "Possible contamination" and subsequently recalculates the total valid sequencing depth for each sample. Because the removal of contaminants shrinks the total sample size, new 'clean' relative abundances are computed to ensure the remaining true detections sum to 100%. Finally, fully zeroed records are removed to optimize the dataset for downstream ecological analyses.

-   **Apply contamination filters and update frequencies.** Zero out contaminants, compute the new clean sample totals, update relative frequencies, and discard empty rows.

    ```{r}
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Clean Abundance` = dplyr::case_when(
        `Contamination status` == "Possible contamination" ~ 0,
        TRUE ~ Abundance)) %>%
      dplyr::group_by(Project, 
                      UniqueFilename, 
                      Primer, 
                      `Read origin`) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Clean Sample Total` = sum(`Clean Abundance`)) %>%
      dplyr::ungroup() %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Clean Relative Abundance` = dplyr::case_when(
        `Clean Sample Total` > 0 ~ `Clean Abundance` / `Clean Sample Total`,
        TRUE ~ 0)) %>%
      dplyr::filter(`Clean Abundance` > 0)
    ```

### Save the Environment!

-   **Save the R environment.** Stores all objects and variables created in this section so you can reload them later if the pipeline fails or you need to resume from this point.

    ```{r}
    base::save.image(paste0(env_path, "/", "9_Contam_analysis-", Sys.Date(), ".RData"))
    ```

## 10. Final Results Curation

Refines BLAST hits into final ecological identifications with confidence scores, taxonomic ranks, expected amplicon lengths, and metazoan categorization for downstream analysis.Refines BLAST hits into final ecological identifications with confidence scores, taxonomic ranks, expected amplicon lengths, and metazoan categorization for downstream analysis.

### Assign Identification Confidence

-   **Calculate confidence scores.** Compute a pseudo-score based on BLAST identity and coverage, and assign taxonomic ranks (Species, Genus, Family, etc.) based on standard percentage thresholds.

    ```{r}
    # Calculate scores and assign taxonomic resolution
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Curated ID` = `Final ID`,
                  # Weighted score: heavily weights identity (10x) over coverage
                  `BLASTn pseudo-score` = ((10 * `1_indentity`) + `1_qcovhsp`) / 11,

    # Assign resolution level based on score thresholds
    Identification = dplyr::case_when(`BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 98 ~ `Final ID`,
                                      `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 95 ~ `Genus (NCBI)`,
                                      `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 90 ~ `Family (NCBI)`,
                                      `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 80 ~ `Order (NCBI)`,
                                      `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 60 ~ `Class (NCBI)`,
                                      TRUE ~ "Unidentified"),

    `Identification Max. taxonomy` = 
      dplyr::case_when(`BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 98 ~ "Species",
                       `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 95 ~ "Genus",
                       `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 90 ~ "Family",
                       `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 80 ~ "Order",
                       `BLASTn pseudo-score` >= 60 ~ "Class",
                       TRUE ~ "Unidentified"))
    ```

### Mark Metazoan Taxa

-   **Categorize Metazoans.** Flag ASVs belonging to Metazoa or other target groups (Zooplankton, Phytoplankton, Benthos) based on their taxonomy. This facilitates filtering for specific ecological guilds.

    ```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
    # 1. Define Biological Groups Lists (Expert Curation)
    # Benthos
    bentos_phy <- c("Annelida", "Arthropoda", "Mollusca")
    bentos_cls <- c("Bivalvia", "Clitellata", "Gastropoda", "Insecta")

    # Zooplankton
    zplnk_phy <- c("Amoebozoa", "Arthropoda", "Cercozoa", "Ciliophora", "Rotifera")
    zplnk_cls <- c("Branchiopoda", "Eurotatoria", "Filosia", "Hexanauplia", 
                   "Lobosa", "Oligohymenophorea")

    # Periphyton
    prftn_phy <- c("Bacillariophyta", "Charophyta", "Chlorophyta", "Cryptophyta",
                   "Cyanobacteria", "Euglenophyta", "Rhodophyta")
    prftn_cls <- c("Bacillariophyceae", "Chlorophyceae", "Chrysophyceae",
                   "Coscinodiscophyceae", "Cryptophyceae", "Cyanophyceae",
                   "Euglenophyceae", "Florideophyceae", "Mediophyceae",
                   "Ulvophyceae", "Zygnematophyceae")

    # Phytoplankton (Comprehensive list)
    ftplnk_phy <- c(
      "Bacillariophyta", "Charophyta", "Chlorophyta", "Cryptophyta", 
      "Cyanobacteria", "Dinophyta", "Euglenophyta", "Ochrophyta",
      "Apicomplexa", "Cercozoa", "Ciliophora", "Discosea", "Endomyxa", 
      "Euglenozoa", "Evosea", "Foraminifera", "Fornicata",
      "Haptophyta", "Hemimastigophora", "Heterolobosea", "Nebulidia", 
      "Nibbleridia", "Oomycota", "Parabasalia", "Perkinsozoa",
      "Preaxostyla", "Rhodophyta", "Tubulinea")
    ftplnk_cls <- c(
      "Bacillariophyceae", "Chlorophyceae", "Chrysophyceae", "Coscinodiscophyceae",
      "Cryptophyceae", "Cyanophyceae", "Dinophyceae", "Euglenophyceae",
      "Mediophyceae", "Trebouxiophyceae", "Ulvophyceae", "Zygnematophyceae",
      "Actinophryidae", "Aphelidea", "Bigyra", "Bolidophyceae", "Breviatea",
      "Centroplasthelida", "Choanoflagellata", "Developea", "Dictyochophyceae",
      "Eustigmatophyceae", "Filasterea", "Glaucocystophyceae", 
      "Hyphochytriomycetes", "Ichthyosporea", "Olisthodiscophyceae", 
      "Pelagophyceae", "Phaeophyceae", "Phaeothamniophyceae", "Raphidophyceae",
      "Synurophyceae", "Xanthophyceae")

    # Combine all target classes and phyla for quick lookup
    all_target_phy <- unique(c(bentos_phy, zplnk_phy, prftn_phy, ftplnk_phy))
    all_target_cls <- unique(c(bentos_cls, zplnk_cls, prftn_cls, ftplnk_cls))

    # 2. Apply Logic to Mark Metazoa/Targets
    all_ps_tbl_final <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      dplyr::mutate(`Possible Metazoa` = dplyr::case_when(
      # Exclusions (Hard filters)
      stringr::str_detect(`blast ID`, 
                          "Chordate environmental") ~ FALSE,
      stringr::str_detect(`blast ID`, 
                          "Match_not_reliable") ~ FALSE,
      stringr::str_detect(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`, 
                          "Bacteria|Archaea|Viruses") ~ FALSE, # Note: Viruses added
      is.na(`Kingdom (NCBI)`) ~ FALSE,
      
      # Inclusions (Target Groups)
      stringr::str_detect(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`, "Eukaryota") & 
        (`Phylum (NCBI)` %in% all_target_phy) ~ TRUE,
      stringr::str_detect(`Superkingdom (NCBI)`, "Eukaryota") & 
        (`Class (NCBI)` %in% all_target_cls) ~ TRUE,
      
      # General Metazoa/Viridiplantae catch-all (if not excluded above)
      stringr::str_detect(`Kingdom (NCBI)`, "Metazoa|Viridiplantae") ~ TRUE,
      
      # Default
      TRUE ~ FALSE))

    # Check distribution
    # table(all_ps_tbl_final$`Possible Metazoa`, useNA = "always")
    ```

### Final Table Formatting

-   **Reorganize columns.** Select and rename columns to produce a clean, user-friendly output table for reporting.

    ```{r, echo=TRUE, eval=FALSE}
    # Create final clean table
    results_table_clean <- all_ps_tbl_final %>%
      # Rename control columns for clarity
      dplyr::rename(`PCR Control` = `PCR control`,
                    `Ext. Control` = `Extraction control`,
                    `Filt. Control` = `Filtration control`
                    ) %>%
      # Select and order essential columns
    ```
